generated_oml:
// C3: Physical Sensing Components
instance MotorPositionEncoderLeft : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Position encoder for left motor providing real-time angular position measurements for MABI MAX 100 robot drive system"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorPositionDataLeft
]

instance MotorPositionEncoderRight : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Position encoder for right motor providing real-time angular position measurements for MABI MAX 100 robot drive system"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorPositionDataRight
]

instance MotorCurrentSensorLeft : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Current sensor monitoring electrical parameters of left motor drive system in MABI MAX 100 robot"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorCurrentDataLeft
]

instance MotorCurrentSensorRight : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Current sensor monitoring electrical parameters of right motor drive system in MABI MAX 100 robot"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorCurrentDataRight
]

// C4: Physical-to-Virtual Interaction
instance MotorPositionDataLeft : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorPositionEncoderLeft
]

instance MotorPositionDataRight : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorPositionEncoderRight
]

instance MotorCurrentDataLeft : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorCurrentSensorLeft
]

instance MotorCurrentDataRight : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorCurrentSensorRight
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance ManufacturingTelemetryData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated contextual data traces from physical system including position, current, and operational logs for manufacturing process monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:fromData MotorPositionDataLeft, MotorPositionDataRight, MotorCurrentDataLeft, MotorCurrentDataRight
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLModelingEnabler, SimulationProcessingEnabler
]

instance SysMLArchitectureModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) and state machines (v1.7) representing logical architecture and component-connector relationships for MABI MAX 100 robot"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLModelingEnabler
]

instance AADLComponentModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "AADL-based structural model describing component-connector dependencies and physical/technical layer architecture of MABI MAX 100 robot system"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLModelingEnabler, SimulationProcessingEnabler
]

instance BehavioralSimulationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink-based behavioral model implementing state machines and automata for MABI MAX 100 robot motion control and performance simulation"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationProcessingEnabler
]

instance PhysicsBasedSimulationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "System dynamics and physics-based simulation model capturing mechanical dynamics, kinematics, and thermal behavior of MABI MAX 100 robot components"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationProcessingEnabler
]

// C11: Enablers
instance SysMLModelingEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML-based modeling toolchain including MontiArc for architecture description and SysML Product Structure Diagrams for geometric representation"
    DTDFVocab:enables ArchitectureModelingService, FunctionalAnalysisService
]

instance SimulationProcessingEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink and domain-specific simulation tools for behavioral modeling and physics-based system validation"
    DTDFVocab:enables PerformanceSimulationService, AnomalyDetectionService
]

instance MQTTDataProtocol : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MQTT-based communication protocol enabling real-time or near-real-time data streaming from physical sensors to digital representation"
    DTDFVocab:enables DataAggregationService
]

instance RFLPMethodology : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) methodology for managing system complexity across abstraction layers from requirements to physical implementation"
    DTDFVocab:enables ModelRefinementService
]

// C6: Services
instance DataAggregationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service aggregating and abstracting contextual data traces from physical system sensors for manufacturing process monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:provides ManufacturingInsight, DataQualityInsight
]

instance ArchitectureModelingService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service creating SysML IBDs and AADL models representing system architecture and component-connector relationships"
    DTDFVocab:provides StructuralInsight
]

instance FunctionalAnalysisService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service analyzing functional architecture using SysML state machines and MontiArc automata for logical behavior validation"
    DTDFVocab:provides BehavioralInsight
]

instance PerformanceSimulationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service executing Simulink-based simulations to generate predictive analytics and performance metrics for MABI MAX 100 robot"
    DTDFVocab:provides PerformanceMetricsInsight, PredictiveAnalyticsInsight
]

instance AnomalyDetectionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service analyzing simulation outputs and real-time data for identifying performance deviations and operational anomalies"
    DTDFVocab:provides AnomalyDetectionInsight
]

instance ModelRefinementService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service iteratively refining digital twin models based on feedback from physical system and simulation validation"
    DTDFVocab:provides ModelValidationInsight
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance ManufacturingInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Contextualized insights about manufacturing process performance derived from aggregated sensor data and simulation outputs"
]

]

]

]

    base:desc "Identification of performance deviations and operational anomalies through comparative analysis of simulation and real-time data"
]

instance ModelValidationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Validation results assessing model fidelity through simulation accuracy and data consistency verification"
]

// C1: Environment
instance ManufacturingEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:desc "High-precision industrial manufacturing environment hosting MABI MAX 100 robot system with closed-loop control requirements"
    base:contains MABIMAX100RobotSystem
]

instance MABIMAX100RobotSystem : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "MABI MAX 100 industrial robot designed for machining operations with complex multi-physics system requiring integrated modeling of mechanical, electrical, and control subsystems"
    base:isContainedIn ManufacturingEnvironment
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the described Digital Twin system is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. However, the distinction between **digital shadows** (passive, one-way data flows) and **Digital Twins** (active, bidirectional) implies that a DT system may include **control loops** or **feedback mechanisms** to influence physical systems. While no specific **command types**, **control message formats**, or **validation/authentication mechanisms** are mentioned, the broader context of DTs (e.g., [35]–[37]) suggests potential use of **standardized IoT protocols** (e.g., MQTT for bidirectional communication) or **API-based control interfaces**. Feedback systems could involve **real-time monitoring** of physical states (e.g., via sensor data) to trigger corrective actions, though conflict resolution strategies (e.g., priority-based or rule-based) are not specified. Command execution confirmation is implied but not described in technical detail. **Not in Document** for precise technical specifications."
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system described in the documents does not explicitly detail synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or specific time scales for twinning operations. However, the Digital Shadow Reference Model (as referenced in [32]) implies a one-way data flow from the physical system to the digital representation, where data traces are aggregated and processed asynchronously. The temporal granularity of data processing is determined by the underlying data calculation or simulation models (e.g., MathWorks Simulink [28] or other simulation tools [29], [30]), which define the frequency and timing of computations. No explicit real-time constraints or periodic synchronization intervals (e.g., millisecond-level updates) are mentioned in the provided context. The processing components consume input data traces and produce output traces, but the exact temporal alignment between physical and digital components remains unspecified beyond the one-way flow described in [32] and [33]."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The **Digital Twin constellation** is orchestrated via a **reference model-based approach**, where the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** [32] serves as a foundational framework for concretization into domain-specific models [41]. The system integrates **heterogeneous modeling tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink [28], general-purpose languages like C++/Java) and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** to describe components, interfaces, and interconnections [23]–[27]. Processing components perform **data aggregation, abstraction, and calculation** using simulation or data models, with **traceability links** ensuring end-to-end data flow [16]. Orchestration relies on **conformance relations** between reference concepts and concrete elements (e.g., UML Class Diagrams) [41], and **purpose-driven design** (e.g., injection molding use cases [42]) tailors the constellation to specific objectives. Resource management is implied through **modular processing components** and **data trace associations**, but no explicit **orchestration patterns** (e.g., event-driven, service-oriented) or **resource allocation strategies** (e.g., load balancing) are detailed."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, encompassing multiple abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. The lifecycle follows a **multi-phase approach** where each phase employs distinct modeling notations and tools:
  - **Requirements Phase**: Defines system objectives and constraints using structured requirements engineering techniques (e.g., SysML requirements diagrams).
  - **Functional Architecture Phase**: Describes high-level system functions and interactions via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, **ACME**, or **AADL**, focusing on component-connector perspectives.
  - **Logical Architecture Phase**: Refines functional decomposition into **logical components** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7 or **MontiArc Automaton**) and their behavioral specifications.
  - **Product/Technical Layer**: Integrates **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**, domain-specific simulators) and **general-purpose programming languages** (C++, Java) for detailed behavioral modeling and execution.

**Transition Management** between phases is governed by **methodologies like RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, or **SMArDT**, ensuring consistency across abstraction levels. **Lifecycle governance** involves **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** (e.g., engineers, domain experts) and **toolchain interoperability**, where models in disparate notations (SysML, ADLs, simulation tools) are harmonized via **model transformation frameworks** or **API-mediated data exchange**. No explicit mention of **version control** or **governance frameworks** (e.g., ISO/IEC standards) is provided in the document.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system adheres to **multi-layered abstraction** in modeling, aligning with MBSE methodologies that split descriptions into **requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers**. Fidelity is implicitly addressed through **deviation metrics**—comparing simulated data versus measured data—to assess accuracy. However, explicit **validation and verification (V&V) methods**, **uncertainty quantification**, or **error correction mechanisms** are not detailed. The document focuses on **structural mapping** and **model identification** rather than formal quality assurance processes (e.g., ISO/IEC standards, real-time anomaly detection)."
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "The technical connection for this system is primarily defined by the **unidirectional data flow** between the physical object and its digital shadow, as described in the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]. While the document does not specify network protocols beyond the implied use of **MQTT-like streaming** (inferred from IoT context), it highlights that the system relies on **passive data collection** without bidirectional control. No details are provided on network topology, bandwidth, latency, or redundancy mechanisms. Security protocols or reliability assurances are not mentioned beyond the passive nature of the digital shadow."
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system integrates with external systems through standardized data exchange protocols, as evidenced by the mention of time-series sensor data streamed via **MQTT** (Section IV.A.4, implied by the context of sensor data aggregation). The system leverages interoperability standards to facilitate communication between the digital twin and external enterprise systems or other digital twins, though no specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, REST APIs) or integration patterns (e.g., API gateways, message brokers) are explicitly detailed beyond MQTT. The processing components (e.g., data calculation or simulation models) consume input traces from external sources (e.g., sensors) and produce output traces, enabling integration with broader cyber-physical system (CPS) ecosystems. The system’s design supports heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives [31] and modular tool integration (e.g., SysML, Simulink), but the exact dependencies on external systems or enterprise-level integration patterns (e.g., ESB, microservices) are not specified."
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system adheres to **select industry standards and modeling frameworks**, primarily within the **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** domain:

1. **Modeling Standards**:
   - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: Used for **functional and logical architecture modeling**, including **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for component-connector representations and **state machines** for behavioral specification (SysML v1.7).
   - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**:
     - **ACME (Architecture Description Language)**: For **component-based system modeling**.
     - **AADL (Avionics and Automotive Open System Architecture Language)**: For **logical and technical architecture** with support for **real-time systems** and **safety-critical applications** (though **DO-178C compliance** is not mentioned).
   - **UML (Unified Modeling Language)**: Employed for **Digital Shadow reference modeling** (Fig. 1), particularly in **class diagrams** to define conceptual relationships.

2. **Interoperability and Compliance**:
   - **No Explicit Interoperability Standards**: The document does not reference **ISO/IEC 42010 (Architecture Viewpoints)**, **IEC 62264 (Enterprise-Control System Integration)**, or **OPC UA** for DT interoperability.
   - **No Certification Requirements**: While AADL is commonly used in **avionics and automotive** domains (e.g., **DO-178C** for software safety), the text does not confirm compliance with **specific certification frameworks**.
   - **Toolchain Standards**: Integration with **MathWorks Simulink** and **general-purpose languages (C++, Java)** suggests adherence to **industry-standard simulation protocols**, but no **formal interoperability middleware** (e.g., **ROS 2**, **DDS**) is detailed.

3. **Compliance Frameworks**:
   - The MBSE approach inherently aligns with **ISO/IEC 15288 (Systems Engineering Lifecycle)** and **ISO 10303 (STEP)** for **product data representation**, though these are not explicitly named. No **DT-specific standards** (e.g., **IEEE P2414**, **ISO/TS 23247**) are referenced.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system incorporates **safety-critical design constraints** as evidenced in the context of robotic systems. Specifically, the **safety cell** of the robotic arm (e.g., MABI robot) restricts the maximum working range to **[-90°°, 90°°]**, ensuring operational boundaries are enforced to prevent collisions or unsafe movements. While the provided documents do not explicitly detail broader **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., TLS/SSL for MQTT, access control mechanisms, or risk assessment frameworks), the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework (as referenced in [46] and [50]) implies a structured approach to **semantic asset management**, which could theoretically underpin data integrity and authorization policies. However, no explicit mention of **authentication systems**, **fail-safe mechanisms**, or **compliance with cybersecurity standards** (e.g., IEC 62443) is present in the documents. The **RAMI 4.0 architecture** ([49]) is referenced but does not provide granular details on security implementations beyond its conceptual layers. Thus, while **safety constraints** for physical operations are documented, **security measures** remain unspecified in the provided sources."
]
